body_txt: Surprise — a monster! But you have time to recover from the shock of its appearance because it won't attack until it has posed dramatically and let out a Mighty Roar . Roaring is like a monster's hello. They must become hostile when heroes don't have the decency to roar a proper return greeting. Often used as a cliffhanger to confrontations because ending after a punch is thrown seems an interruption, while a warcry is the last stop to get off at before the fight starts. Although this trope is Truth in Television , it's not employed nearly as often in real life as you'd expect. In the majority of cases, an animal that roars is warning you away. It doesn't want to fight you; it wants to scare you off. An animal that tries to sneak up on you is much more likely to harm you, and making loud noises prior to attacking would ruin the element of surprise; hence, most animals that do roar when attacking stay completely silent until the moment they finally ambush their prey. However, animals will roar or scream when fighting others of their own species in competitive or territorial bouts, as in this case surprise isn't always required. Contrast Hiss Before Fleeing . Compare Transformation Name Announcement , Screaming Warrior , and In the Name of the Moon , which are often the tropes you get when the hero does this. A similar trope for enemies capable of speech is Simultaneous Warning and Action . If the crucial element of surprise is blown by this it may be a case of With Catlike Tread . If the roar is a recurring roar and unique to the monster, it's a Signature Roar . If you thought "beating" meant "defeat", you may be looking for Pre-Mortem One-Liner . Not to be confused with Primal Chest-Pound , which about primates beating on "themselves" while roaring (though the two may overlap, especially if the primate in question is a Killer Gorilla ). Sub-Trope of Noisy Nature , assuming it's a Real Life animal.
